metadata.title: EMA Guideline on the environmental risk assessment of medicinal products for human use (Rev. 1-Corr.)
metadata.note: Replaces EMEA/CHMP/SWP/4447/00 corr 2. Adopted by CHMP 15 February 2024, in effect 1 September 2024. Covers ERA for all human medicinal products submitted via centralised, mutual recognition, decentralised or national procedures. 64 pages.

rules.rule_text: An Environmental Risk Assessment (ERA) is mandatory for all new marketing authorisation applications (MAAs) for medicinal products for human use submitted through any procedure (centralised, mutual recognition, decentralised, or national). The ERA must be provided in Module 1.6 of the eCTD dossier. Any missing data or studies must be justified by the applicant.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Scope and legal basis; Section 9
rules.verbatim_quote: An ERA is required for all new MAAs for a medicinal product submitted through a centralised, mutual recognition, decentralised or national procedure... The environmental risk assessment should be provided in Module 1.6 of the MAA.
exceptions: Renewals of marketing authorisations do not require an ERA

rules.rule_text: For Type II variations, the ERA dossier must be updated if an anticipated increase in environmental exposure is expected (e.g. new indication, increased dose, new route of administration, or application in a member state with higher disease prevalence). For extension applications per Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1085/2003, an ERA is also required when increased environmental exposure is anticipated.
rules.verbatim_quote: For type II variations, the ERA dossier should be updated if there is an anticipated increase in the environmental exposure, e.g. a new indication which results in an increase in the extent of the use.

rules.rule_text: The ERA must be performed for each pharmacologically active substance in the product. For fixed combination products, the ERA is performed separately for each active substance. Excipients are out of scope of this guideline.
rules.verbatim_quote: All pharmacologically active substances in the product need to undergo an ERA. For fixed combination products, the ERA is performed separately for each active substance within the product. Excipients are out of scope of the guideline.

rules.rule_text: All ERA studies generated by or on behalf of the applicant must be compliant with Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) where applicable, and must preferably follow the most recent OECD or comparable international validated test guidelines. QSARs and read-across cannot replace experimental ERA studies required by this guideline. Non-standard test methods must be justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Data generated by or on behalf of the applicant in order to meet the ERA data requirements specified in this guideline should be compliant with Good Laboratory Practice (GLP) where applicable and preferably follow the most recent test guidelines issued by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)... Quantitative structure-activity relationships (QSARs) and read-across currently cannot replace the studies requested in this guideline.
exceptions: 3Rs principles encouraged to develop validated alternative assays to potentially replace in vivo assays

rules.rule_text: Public Assessment Reports (PARs/EPARs) and summary data from other regulatory frameworks cannot be used as data in the ERA dossier. Endpoints owned by another company require a letter of access plus the original study reports to be submitted.
rules.verbatim_quote: (European) Public Assessment Reports (PARs and EPARs) and reviews or summary data from other regulatory frameworks cannot be used as data in the ERA dossier. Endpoints are owned by the company who submitted them in the original procedure and cannot be used by other applicants without a letter of access.

rules.rule_text: Phase I Risk Assessment uses a decision tree to calculate the Predicted Environmental Concentration in surface water (PEC_SW). The default PEC_SW is calculated using: (a) maximum daily dose of active substance; (b) default market penetration factor F_PEN = 0.01 (1% of population receiving the substance daily); (c) default wastewater volume = 200 L per inhabitant per day; and (d) default dilution factor = 10. If PEC_SW ≥ 0.01 μg L⁻¹, Phase II risk assessment must be performed.
rules.verbatim_quote: In Phase I, the PEC calculation is restricted to the surface water compartment... 1% of a population receives the active substance daily... If the PEC_SW value is < 0.01 μg L⁻¹ and no other environmental concerns are apparent, it is assumed that the medicinal product is unlikely to represent a risk to the environment.
exceptions: EAS and antiparasitics must proceed to Phase II regardless of PEC_SW (action limit does not apply)

rules.rule_text: Endocrine active substances (EAS) and antiparasitics (excluding antiprotozoals) must always enter Phase II risk assessment regardless of their PEC_SW value. EAS require a tailored testing strategy addressing their specific mechanism of action. Antibacterials require a tailored testing strategy only if PEC_SW exceeds the action limit.
rules.verbatim_quote: Endocrine active substances: Enter Phase II regardless of PEC — Yes; Tailored testing — Yes. Antibacterials: Enter Phase II regardless of PEC — No; Tailored testing — Yes. Antiparasitics: Enter Phase II regardless of PEC — Yes; Tailored testing — No.
exceptions: Antiprotozoals are exempted from mandatory Phase II entry

rules.rule_text: Phase II Tier A risk assessment requires the following mandatory physico-chemical, fate, and ecotoxicity studies: water solubility (OECD 105); octanol/water partitioning (OECD 107 or 123); adsorption to soil and sludge (OECD 106, 2 sludge types + 3 soil types); ready biodegradability (OECD 301); aquatic toxicity — algae growth inhibition (OECD 201), Daphnia reproduction (OECD 211), fish early life stage (OECD 210); STP activated sludge respiration inhibition (OECD 209). EC₁₀ values are preferred over NOECs.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.1 — Phase II Tier A studies (Table 2)
rules.verbatim_quote: The base set of data cannot be omitted even if studies such as OECD 303A and OECD 314B show degradation in STPs... Experimental studies should preferably follow the test guidelines issued by the OECD or the European Commission.
exceptions: For EAS, fish early life stage test (OECD 210) should be replaced by more sensitive tests based on MoA

rules.rule_text: Aquatic ecotoxicity data must be chronic (NOEC or EC₁₀) for three trophic levels (algae, Daphnia, fish) because pharmaceutical residue emission to surface water is continuous. When a reliable concentration-response curve is observed, EC₁₀ is preferred over NOEC for PNEC derivation, even if EC₁₀ is numerically higher. Concentrations must be measured analytically; nominal concentrations acceptable only if within 80–120% of measured.
rules.verbatim_quote: To determine the aquatic ecotoxicity, chronic ecotoxicity data i.e. No Observed Effect Concentration (NOEC) or 10% effect concentration (EC₁₀) for species from three trophic levels is required... the EC₁₀ is preferred over the NOEC for PNEC derivation, even if the former is higher than the latter.

rules.rule_text: The PNEC for surface water (PNEC_SW) is calculated by applying an assessment factor (AF) of 10 to the lowest EC₁₀ or NOEC from the three trophic level aquatic test species. The surface water risk quotient (RQ_SW = PEC_SW / PNEC_SW) must be <1 to conclude no risk. If RQ_SW ≥ 1, Phase II Tier B refinement is required.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.3.1 — Phase II Tier A surface water
rules.verbatim_quote: The PNEC_SW is calculated by applying an assessment factor (AF) of 10 to the lowest EC₁₀ or to the effect concentration at which 10% effect for inhibition of growth rate is observed... If the surface water RQ is ≥1, a Phase II Tier B assessment is required.

rules.rule_text: Soil compartment assessment is triggered by combined K_FOC,SLUDGE and PEC_SW thresholds per Table 4: (1) K_FOC,SLUDGE >10,000 L kg⁻¹ — soil assessment required regardless of PEC_SW; (2) 5,000 ≤ K_FOC,SLUDGE ≤ 10,000 and PEC_SW ≥ 1 μg L⁻¹; (3) 2,500 ≤ K_FOC,SLUDGE < 5,000 and PEC_SW ≥ 2 μg L⁻¹; (4) 1,000 ≤ K_FOC,SLUDGE < 2,500 and PEC_SW ≥ 3 μg L⁻¹.
rules.verbatim_quote: Table 4: Combined trigger values for substances entering a Phase II risk assessment for soil compartment and/or groundwater.
exceptions: Soil assessment not required when substance is readily biodegradable

rules.rule_text: Groundwater assessment via bank filtration is required when K_FOC,SLUDGE ≤ 10,000 L kg⁻¹ (unless readily biodegradable). The groundwater PEC is calculated as PEC_GW = 0.25 × PEC_SW. The PNEC_GW is derived by applying an additional AF of 10 to PNEC_SW (i.e. PNEC_GW = PNEC_SW / 10), reflecting the greater vulnerability of groundwater ecosystems.
rules.verbatim_quote: PEC_GW = 0.25 · PEC_SW (Eq. 27)... Groundwater ecosystems are fundamentally different to surface water ecosystems and therefore more vulnerable... an additional AF of 10 should be applied to extrapolate the PNEC_GW from the PNEC_SW (Equation 28).

rules.rule_text: Secondary poisoning assessment is required when log K_ow (or log D_ow for dissociating compounds) ≥ 3. A bioconcentration factor in fish (BCF_FISH) must be determined experimentally using OECD 305, normalised to 5% lipid content. If BCF_FISH ≥ 100 L kg⁻¹, a full secondary poisoning risk assessment is required. If BCF_FISH > 2,000 L kg⁻¹, the PBT B-criterion is considered fulfilled.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the log K_ow (or log D_ow for dissociating compounds) is ≥ 3... a bioconcentration factor (BCF) needs to be determined experimentally. If this BCF is ≥ 100 L kg⁻¹, a secondary poisoning assessment is requested.

rules.rule_text: For antibacterials with no other pharmacological targets, a tailored testing strategy must be applied using: Anabaena flos-aquae (cyanobacteria, OECD 201); Synechococcus leopoliensis (cyanobacteria, OECD 201); Raphidocelis subcapitata (green algae, OECD 201); activated sludge respiration inhibition (OECD 209); and Daphnia magna reproduction (OECD 211). Fish tests are not required for antibacterials.
rules.verbatim_quote: For active substances with an antibacterial MoA and no other known pharmacological targets, a tailored and targeted effect assessment should be performed for the aquatic compartment... fish tests are not required.

rules.rule_text: An active substance must be identified as an endocrine active substance (EAS) if there is evidence it can affect development or reproduction by interacting with oestrogen, androgen, thyroid, or other steroid hormone receptors. EAS identification criteria include: in vitro EC₅₀/IC₅₀ < 10 μM at steroid hormone receptors; in vivo endocrine-related adverse effects at LOAEL; or scientific literature evidence of intersex, feminisation/masculinisation, or developmental effects in aquatic organisms.
rules.verbatim_quote: If there is evidence that the active substance can exert an effect on development or reproduction by directly interacting or interfering with receptors, hormone levels or activities of oestrogens, androgens, thyroid hormones or other steroid hormones, that active substance should be assessed in Phase II regardless of the PEC.

rules.rule_text: For EAS, the recommended effect tests depend on mechanism of action (Table 17): Oestrogen receptor agonist → fish full lifecycle test (OECD 240); Oestrogen receptor antagonist → fish sexual development test (OECD 234) or OECD 240; Androgen receptor agonist → OECD 234 or OECD 240; Androgen receptor antagonist → fish full lifecycle test (OECD 240); Aromatase inhibitor → OECD 234 or OECD 240; Thyroid hormone agonists/antagonists → larval amphibian growth and development assay (OECD 241).
rules.verbatim_quote: Table 17: Overview of recommended effect studies assessing apical endpoints for active substances with different endocrine mechanism of action and thyroid hormone agonist and antagonists.
exceptions: PNEC derived by applying AF of 10 to lowest EC10 or NOEC for population-relevant endpoints

rules.rule_text: A PBT/vPvB screening assessment must be performed for all active substances. The screening consists of: (1) confirming the substance is not naturally occurring; (2) confirming no prior PBT assessment exists for the same substance; (3) determining whether the substance is a non-natural peptide/protein that is readily biodegradable; and (4) determining log K_ow. If log K_ow > 4.5, a definitive PBT/vPvB assessment must be performed.
rules.verbatim_quote: A PBT/vPvB screening assessment should be performed for all active substances... When the trigger value of log K_ow >4.5 is met, a definitive PBT/vPvB assessment should be performed.
exceptions: Naturally occurring substances may justify no further PBT assessment

rules.rule_text: A substance meets the PBT Persistence criterion (P) if any of the following: degradation half-life in marine water >60 days; in fresh/estuarine water >40 days; in marine sediment >180 days; in fresh/estuarine water sediment >120 days; or in soil >120 days. A substance meets the vPvB very-persistent criterion (vP) if half-life in marine, fresh, or estuarine water >60 days; in marine, fresh, or estuarine water sediment >180 days; or in soil >180 days.
rules.verbatim_quote: Table 18: PBT and vPvB criteria... (a) the degradation half-life in fresh or estuarine water is longer than 40 days... vP: (a) the degradation half-life in marine, fresh or estuarine water is longer than 60 days.

rules.rule_text: A substance meets the PBT Bioaccumulation criterion (B) when BCF in aquatic species > 2,000 L kg⁻¹. A substance meets the vPvB very-bioaccumulative criterion (vB) when BCF in aquatic species > 5,000 L kg⁻¹. BCF values must be normalised to 5% lipid content with correction for growth dilution per OECD 305.
rules.verbatim_quote: A substance fulfils the bioaccumulation criterion (B) when the bioconcentration factor in aquatic species is higher than 2,000. [vB]: when the bioconcentration factor in aquatic species is higher than 5,000.

rules.rule_text: A substance meets the PBT Toxicity criterion (T) if any of the following apply: (a) long-term NOEC or EC₁₀ for marine or freshwater organisms < 0.01 mg/L; (b) the substance is classified as carcinogenic (Cat. 1A or 1B), germ cell mutagenic (Cat. 1 or 1B), or toxic for reproduction (Cat. 1A, 1B, or 2) under Regulation (EC) No 1272/2008; or (c) there is evidence of chronic toxicity — STOT RE category 1 or 2.
rules.verbatim_quote: A substance fulfils the toxicity criterion (T) in any of the following situations: (a) the long-term no-observed effect concentration (NOEC) or EC₁₀ for marine or freshwater organisms is less than 0.01 mg/L.
exceptions: CLP classification criteria (b) and (c) apply only to chemicals classified under REACH, not directly to human pharmaceuticals in preclinical hazard assessment

rules.rule_text: Persistence studies for PBT assessment must reflect European environmental temperatures and be conducted preferably at 12°C. If studies are conducted at different temperatures, DT₅₀ values must be extrapolated to 12°C using the Arrhenius equation with default activation energy E_A = 65,400 J mol⁻¹. Non-extractable residues (NER) that are potentially reversible (e.g. physically entrapped) must not be treated as degradation in persistence assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: Persistence studies should reflect environmental temperatures in Europe and therefore preferably be conducted at 12°C... The formation of non-extractable residues (NER) should not per se be considered as degradation.

rules.rule_text: When a risk is identified (RQ ≥ 1 in any compartment) or a substance is classified as PBT/vPvB, this information must be included in the Summary of Product Characteristics (SmPC) (sections 5.3 and 6.6) and in the patient leaflet (PL, section 5). Risk mitigation measures and disposal instructions must be proposed and discussed.
rules.verbatim_quote: When a risk is identified and/or a substance is classified as PBT/vPvB, this information should be included in the summary of product characteristics (SmPC), and risk mitigation measures should be discussed.

rules.rule_text: All medicinal products — even those that do not require special disposal measures — must include patient leaflet language advising not to dispose of medicines via wastewater or household waste, and to consult a pharmacist on disposal. When a risk has been identified by the ERA, SmPC section 6.6 must additionally state 'This medicinal product may pose a risk to the environment.'
rules.verbatim_quote: It is therefore recommended that even medicinal products that do not require special disposal measures are appropriately labelled. See Table 20... 'This medicinal product may pose a risk to the environment.'

rules.rule_text: All ERA studies must be assessed for reliability using a standardised method such as the CRED method. Only studies with reliability score 1 (reliable) or 2 (reliable with restrictions) may be used in the risk assessment. Studies with reliability score 3 (unreliable) or 4 (not assignable) cannot be used.
rules.verbatim_quote: A standardised assessment method designed for toxicological/ecotoxicological studies, such as the CRED method (Moermond et al, 2016), is therefore recommended... If the CRED method is used, all studies with reliability scores of 1 (reliable) and 2 (reliable with restrictions) should be used for the risk assessment. Studies with reliability 3 (unreliable) or 4 (not assignable due to lack of information) cannot be used.

rules.rule_text: The ERA report (in Module 1.6) must start with clear identification of the active substance including: company name/code, IUPAC name, CAS number, empirical formula, structural formula, SMILES code, and molecular weight. The report must contain summaries of all studies used, and a dated signature from the author including their educational, training, and occupational experience, and their relationship with the applicant.
rules.source_section: Section 9 — Structure of the ERA report
rules.verbatim_quote: The ERA report should start with a clear identification of the active substance, including company name/code, IUPAC name, CAS number, empirical formula, structural formula, SMILES code, and molecular weight... A dated signature of the author, information on the author's relevant educational, training and occupational experience, and a statement of the author's relationship with the applicant, shall be included.

rules.rule_text: The 'total residue approach' assumes the active substance is completely excreted as the parent compound without metabolism. For prodrugs with <50% conversion to active metabolite (>50% excreted intact), the selection of substances for ERA must be justified with scientific advice from regulatory agencies. Metabolites constituting ≥10% of administered dose require a full Phase II risk assessment if the total residue approach is abandoned.
rules.verbatim_quote: The 'total residue approach' assumes that the active substance is completely excreted as parent compound without metabolism... a full Phase II risk assessment is required for each excreted metabolite constituting ≥10% of the administered dose.

rules.rule_text: For sediment risk assessment (Phase II Tier A), a minimum of one chronic toxicity study with sediment-dwelling organisms using a sediment-water test system is required to derive PNEC_SED. Acceptable tests: Chironomus spiked water/sediment (OECD 218/219), Chironomus life-cycle study (OECD 233), or Lumbriculus sp. (OECD 225). With one chronic sediment test, AF of 100 applies; with two tests from different conditions, AF of 50 applies.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.4.1 — Phase II Tier A for sediment
rules.verbatim_quote: To determine a PNEC_SED, a minimum of one toxicity study with sediment dwelling organisms should be performed using a sediment-water test system... If data from a single chronic sediment test is available, an AF of 100 should be applied to the EC₁₀ or NOEC in order to derive the PNEC.

rules.rule_text: For soil risk assessment (Phase II Tier A), four ecotoxicity tests at different trophic levels are required: nitrogen transformation test in soil organisms (OECD 216, 28-day); terrestrial plants — six species from six different families including four dicotyledonous species (OECD 208); earthworm/enchytraeid (OECD 222/OECD 220); and Collembola (OECD 232). PNEC_SOIL is derived by applying AF of 10 to the lowest EC₁₀ or NOEC. An OECD 307 transformation in soil study is required for persistence assessment.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.6.1 — Tier A Effect Assessment for soil (Table 11)
rules.verbatim_quote: Four tests on different trophic levels are required for the soil compartment, including a functional test with soil microorganisms, and ecotoxicological tests with soil-dwelling invertebrates and plant species... The PNEC_SOIL is calculated by applying an AF of 10 to the lowest EC₁₀ or NOEC value from the soil test species.

rules.rule_text: The market penetration factor (F_PEN) may be refined using European disease prevalence data from a reliable, independent published source (peer-reviewed journal or WHO). Prevalence data must not be older than 5 years (with justification if older). For orphan drugs, F_PEN may be based on the COMP-designated prevalence. The total PEC_SW for multi-indication products is the sum of PEC_SW values calculated separately for each indication.
rules.verbatim_quote: F_PEN can be refined by submitting European disease prevalence data for the sought indication(s). Such data should be published by a reliable and independent source... preferably not older than 5 years.

rules.rule_text: The outcome of the ERA must not constitute a criterion for refusal of a marketing authorisation under current EU requirements per Directive 2001/83/EC Article 8(3). However, identified environmental risks must be reflected in the SmPC and patient leaflet, and risk mitigation measures must be proposed. Precautionary and safety measures are to be included in both the SmPC and patient leaflet.
rules.verbatim_quote: In accordance with Article 8(3) of Directive 2001/83/EC, as amended, the potential environmental risks posed by the use of medicinal products shall be evaluated and, on a case-by-case basis, specific arrangements to limit this risk shall be considered. Under the current requirements, the outcome of the ERA should not constitute a criterion for refusal of a marketing authorisation.
